ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for braised duck feet; mapped to the closest parent class 'duck food product' (FOODON_03302050) as an approximate anchor.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a specific code for braised duck feet; A0FKP (Duck meat, cooked) is the closest defensible parent node. Duck feet are a collagen-rich extremity product distinct from muscle meat.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for braised duck feet as a specific food concept.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of braised duck feet in Singapore is approximately 120 g, equivalent to 3–4 duck feet, commonly served as a side dish or appetiser at zi char stalls and Chinese restaurants.

nutrient_source.title: Braised Duck Feet — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Input record carries HPB provenance at confidence 0.95 for all per_100g fields. Values are accepted as-is from the HPB Foodo database entry for braised duck feet. Saturated fat is estimated from USDA FoodData Central duck meat cooked data (NDB 05143) as HPB does not separately report saturated fat for this item.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson et al.; analogous estimate for high-protein, very-low-carbohydrate braised meat dish
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for braised duck feet. Carbohydrate content is very low at 1.2 g/100 g, derived almost entirely from soy sauce sugars. GI is estimated at approximately 15 (low) by analogy with other high-protein, very-low-carbohydrate braised meat dishes reported in the University of Sydney GI Database and peer-reviewed GI tables. Glycemic load per 120 g serving = (15 × 1.44 g available carbohydrate) / 100 = 0.22, rounded to 0.2.

hypertension.key_factor: Extremely high sodium content at 1050 mg per 100 g
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should avoid or strictly limit braised duck feet due to the sodium content of 1050 mg/100 g, which far exceeds the 600 mg/100 g threshold; a single 120 g serving delivers approximately 1260 mg sodium, representing over half the recommended daily limit of 2000 mg.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very low carbohydrate (1.2 g/100 g) with negligible glycaemic impact, offset by high sodium risk
type2_diabetes.guidance: Braised duck feet have negligible glycaemic impact due to very low carbohydrate content (1.2 g/100 g) and are unlikely to raise blood glucose; however, the extremely high sodium content (1050 mg/100 g) may contribute to fluid retention and indirectly impair insulin sensitivity, so portion size should be strictly limited.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High sodium (1050 mg/100 g) and moderate fat (9.8 g/100 g) with estimated saturated fat contribution
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should avoid or strictly limit braised duck feet because the very high sodium content (1050 mg/100 g) significantly elevates blood pressure risk, and the moderate fat content (9.8 g/100 g, estimated saturated fat ~2.8 g/100 g) adds to cardiovascular burden; if consumed, portion size should not exceed 60 g and frequency should be minimal.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Sodium at 1050 mg/100 g far exceeds the 400 mg/100 g RA threshold, promoting joint and tissue swelling
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid braised duck feet as the sodium content of 1050 mg/100 g substantially exceeds the 400 mg/100 g RA threshold and will exacerbate joint inflammation and tissue swelling; no meaningful anti-inflammatory nutrients are present to offset this risk.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Braised duck feet (卤鸭掌; lu ya zhang) is a popular Singaporean Chinese dish in which duck feet are slow-simmered in a fragrant braising liquid of dark soy sauce, light soy sauce, garlic, ginger, star anise, cinnamon, and sugar until deeply flavoured and tender. The dish is a staple of zi char stalls, Chinese seafood restaurants, and hawker centres across Singapore, and is also widely enjoyed in Hong Kong-style dim sum settings. Duck feet are composed primarily of skin, tendons, and cartilage, yielding a gelatinous, sticky texture with a rich, savoury-sweet umami flavour profile. Per 100 g, the dish provides 165 kcal, 18.1 g protein, 9.8 g fat, and only 1.2 g carbohydrate, making it a high-protein, very-low-carbohydrate food. The collagen content is high due to the cartilaginous nature of the feet. Sodium is extremely high at 1050 mg/100 g owing to the soy sauce braising medium, making this dish unsuitable for individuals with hypertension, cardiovascular disease, or rheumatoid arthritis. Calcium is notably present at 82 mg/100 g, contributed by the bone and cartilage matrix. The glycaemic impact is negligible. Dietary fibre is absent. The dish is not halal-certified in most preparations and contains soy allergens.

confidence_reason: All core per_100g nutrient values are sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database at confidence 0.95. The input record carries canonical provenance from HPB with review_status reviewed. Saturated fat is estimated from USDA FoodData Central duck cooked data as a secondary supplement. GI is an analogous estimate documented with approved source basis.
notes: Saturated fat (2.8 g/100 g) is estimated from USDA FoodData Central duck meat cooked (NDB 05143) as HPB does not separately report saturated fat for this item; this is documented in nutrient_source. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1: detailed micronutrient and fatty acid profile data for braised duck feet are not available in the HPB Foodo database or USDA FoodData Central as of 2026. dietary_fibre_g is 0.0 as confirmed by HPB source; duck feet contain no plant-derived fibre. The input dietary_tags included 'plant_based' which is incorrect for a duck-based dish and has been removed. Halal status is set to false as standard braised duck feet preparations in Singapore are not universally halal-certified. GI is an analogous estimate (approximately 15, low) based on the very low carbohydrate content of 1.2 g/100 g; no direct GI measurement for braised duck feet exists in the University of Sydney GI Database.
